diagnosis: Aortic Dissection (Type B) Mimicking Renal Colic
one_liner: A 54-year-old male with hypertension presents with sudden-onset severe left flank pain radiating to the groin, initially evaluated as nephrolithiasis.

stage2.hpi_extension: Patient presents with sudden-onset severe left flank pain that began 3 hours ago while lifting weights at the gym. The pain is sharp, constant, rated 10/10, and radiates to the left groin. He also reports associated nausea and one episode of vomiting. He states the pain is similar to a kidney stone he had 5 years ago. No hematuria. No dysuria or urinary frequency. He also reports a dull ache between his shoulder blades that started around the same time but considers the flank pain more concerning. He denies chest pain, shortness of breath, or leg weakness. He has been taking his blood pressure medications inconsistently. He used cocaine approximately 36 hours ago at a party (eventually admits to this with direct questioning).
stage2.pmh: Hypertension (poorly controlled), history of nephrolithiasis (one episode 5 years ago), anxiety disorder
stage2.medications: Lisinopril 40 mg daily (inconsistent), alprazolam 0.5 mg as needed
stage2.family_history: Father had aortic aneurysm repair at age 60. Mother has hypertension.
stage2.social_history: Smokes 1 pack/day for 20 years. Occasional cocaine use (last use 36 hours ago). Drinks 3-4 beers on weekends. Works as a personal trainer. Single.

vitals.bp: Right arm: 178/104 mmHg; Left arm: 172/100 mmHg

stage2.physical_exam: General: Muscular male in significant distress, pacing and unable to find comfortable position. Diaphoretic. HEENT: Unremarkable. Neck: No JVD. No bruits. Cardiovascular: Mildly tachycardic, regular rhythm. S1/S2 present. No murmurs (no aortic regurgitation murmur in this condition). Blood pressures similar in both arms. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. Abdomen: Significant left flank tenderness. Left costovertebral angle tenderness. Mild left lower quadrant tenderness. No peritoneal signs. A subtle pulsatile quality to deep palpation of the left upper abdomen. Bowel sounds present. Extremities: All pulses palpable but left femoral pulse is slightly diminished compared to right. No edema. Skin: Diaphoretic. No mottling. Neuro: Alert, oriented x4. No focal deficits.

lab_results.result: Trace blood (1+ hematuria), no WBCs, no nitrites, no bacteria
lab_results.clinical_significance: Mild hematuria can occur with both renal colic AND renal malperfusion from this condition — this does NOT confirm nephrolithiasis
Elevated; may indicate renal malperfusion from dissection flap compromising renal artery
Markedly elevated; D-dimer >500 ng/mL has >95% sensitivity for acute this condition
Mildly elevated, may reflect end-organ malperfusion
Normal (this condition dissection typically does not involve coronary ostia)
Recent cocaine use is a significant risk factor for this condition
Elevated, may reflect end-organ ischemia from malperfusion

imaging_results.test_name: CT Abdomen/Pelvis without contrast (initially ordered for 'renal colic')
imaging_results.result: No renal calculi identified. Left kidney shows diminished cortical enhancement pattern. Incidental note: abnormal caliber of the abdominal aorta with suspicious intimal irregularity — recommend CTA.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: No stone found despite classic presentation; abnormal aortic finding prompts CTA

imaging_results.result: Intimal flap originating distal to the left subclavian artery extending to the aortic bifurcation. The dissection flap involves the left renal artery ostium with diminished left renal perfusion. True lumen compression in the infrarenal segment. No involvement of the ascending aorta. Left common iliac artery partially involved. No free rupture or contained rupture.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Stanford this condition this condition with left renal artery malperfusion — this explains the flank pain, hematuria, and elevated creatinine

imaging_results.result: Sinus rhythm at 98 bpm. Left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria. No acute ST changes.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: LVH consistent with chronic poorly controlled hypertension

low_yield_tests: CT without contrast (insufficient for aortic evaluation)

stage5.final_clue: Initial CT for suspected renal colic shows no stone but an abnormal aorta. CTA reveals a this condition this condition extending from the left subclavian to the bifurcation with left renal artery malperfusion. The flank pain was from renal ischemia due to dissection, not nephrolithiasis. Cocaine use and uncontrolled hypertension were predisposing factors.

debrief.classic_presentation: Type B aortic dissection classically presents with sudden interscapular back pain. However, when the dissection flap involves branch vessels, symptoms may mimic the end-organ being compromised: renal artery involvement mimics renal colic (flank pain, hematuria), mesenteric involvement mimics acute abdomen, and iliac involvement causes leg ischemia. This case demonstrates the dangerous mimic of renal colic.
debrief.attending_mode_notes: This is a high-risk diagnostic pitfall. The patient presents with classic renal colic symptoms (sudden flank pain radiating to the groin, nausea, restlessness), mild hematuria, and a history of prior kidney stones — all reinforcing anchoring bias toward nephrolithiasis. Red flags that should prompt broader evaluation include: (1) pain maximal at onset, (2) concurrent back/interscapular pain, (3) poorly controlled hypertension, (4) cocaine use, (5) family history of aortic disease, (6) elevated creatinine disproportionate to the clinical picture, and (7) no stone found on CT. The trace hematuria is from renal malperfusion, not a stone.
debrief.pathophysiology: Type B aortic dissection involves the descending thoracic aorta distal to the left subclavian artery. It occurs when a primary intimal tear allows blood to enter the aortic media, creating a false lumen that propagates distally. When the false lumen compresses branch vessel ostia (static obstruction) or the intimal flap prolapses across a branch vessel (dynamic obstruction), malperfusion syndromes occur. Left renal artery malperfusion causes renal ischemia manifesting as flank pain, hematuria, and AKI — closely mimicking nephrolithiasis.
debrief.treatment_overview: Uncomplicated Type B dissection: medical management with aggressive blood pressure and heart rate control (IV esmolol, then nicardipine or nitroprusside; target SBP 100-120, HR <60). However, THIS case is COMPLICATED (renal malperfusion) and requires intervention: thoracic endovascular aortic repair (TEVAR) to cover the primary entry tear and restore true lumen flow. Renal artery stenting may be needed if perfusion does not improve. Cease all cocaine use permanently. Lifelong antihypertensive therapy and surveillance imaging. ICU admission.
high_yield_pearls: Aortic dissection can mimic renal colic when the dissection flap involves the renal arteries — always consider dissection in flank pain with no stone on CT.
Cocaine is a major risk factor for aortic dissection in young patients (causes acute hypertensive surges and direct vascular injury).
Complicated Type B dissection (malperfusion, rupture, refractory pain/hypertension) requires intervention (TEVAR), unlike uncomplicated Type B which is medical.
D-dimer <500 ng/mL has excellent negative predictive value (>95%) for acute aortic dissection within the first 24 hours.
Type B dissection does NOT cause aortic regurgitation (the aortic valve is in the ascending aorta).
sources: 2022 ACC/AHA Guideline for Diagnosis and Management of Aortic Disease
Nienaber CA et al. NEJM 2013;369:2126-2136 (INSTEAD-XL trial)

after_labs_top3: Aortic dissection (Type B) with renal malperfusion
